_metadata.description: A collection of GitHub issues for the stbl repository.
_metadata.comment: Each key in the 'issues' object is a string representation of the GitHub issue number. Empty objects are placeholders so that positions and ids match. Empty objects should be ignored.

8.title: `named` arg

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

8.body:
Add an argument to all `stabilize_*()` functions, `named`, with default value `NULL` or maybe `NA`. Its behavior depends on its value:

- `NULL` or `NA` or `character(0)`: Don't check names.
- `TRUE`: Object must be named, but we don't care what the values are.
- `FALSE`: Object must not be named.
- character vector: Object must have these names. Technically it's "the object must `rlang::is_named2(x), and all names of object must be in this vector", because we aren't checking length specifically via this argument.

I don't like that I cant stabilize this argument, but this is the best pattern I can think of for interpretation.

26.title: stabilize_names()

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

26.body: A more complete stabilizer that can confirm precise names, or no duplicates, or that certain names are present, etc.

44.title: Export stabilize_cls()?

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

44.body:
As a developer, I'd like to implement a stbl-style checker for my fancy new class, so that users can get informative error messages for broken bits.

See how necessary this is after finishing vctrs setup.

53.title: Separate Condition Messages from Signal

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

53.body: Errors don't have to be super fast; they'll stop before repeating, so the time won't build up. Focus on making them complete. Collect messages about all possible errors, THEN signal the condition.
comments: I don't want to implement this, at least not how I was thinking here.

Rationale for not doing it: Erroring doesn't have to be fast, but getting through the checks should be. I intentionally code this with a sort of "stop if you fail, return the good thing if you don't" philosophy; I'd have to deal with the returns everywhere to see if they have an error in them, and that would get slow and messy.

Re-consider this specifically for better compatibility with S7. S7 validation should return a character vector with all the reasons for failure, as a sort of incomplete sentence. It might be possible to use `withCallingHandlers()` in a wrapper in order to collect all the would-be error messages (although I think "downstream" steps might fail in unexpected ways if we plow through errors, so I'm not sure).

It might also make sense to make specific versions of `stabilize_*()` and `to_*()` that are focused on this. Maybe `validate_*()` since they'd specifically be used within (S7) validation.

66.title: Quotes around bad values in to_fct

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

66.body: There's a cli decorator to automatically put `" "` around the `Bad values:` list. Do that.

80.title: JSON schema tix
80.body: Make tickets for all the json schéma-recognized field types. That's really what this package is about, lean into it. Some might involve packages in Suggests (eg, don't import clock/lubridate if people don't care about dates, probably something similar for urls).

104.title: Stabilize dates

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

104.body: Add `to_date()` etc. Will probably use `base::as.Date()`, but maybe check for other date packages (clock, lubridate) when coercion is necessary.

105.title: Stabilize date-times

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

105.body: Add `to_dttm()` etc. Will probably use `base::as.POSIXct()`, but maybe check for other date packages (clock, lubridate) when coercion is necessary.

110.title: Implement `stabilize_lst()`

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

110.body:
(Issue created via conversation with a stbl "expert" gem on Google Gemini)

## Overview

We should add a `stabilize_lst()` function to `{stbl}`. The primary purpose of this function would be to validate the structure and contents of lists, which is a common requirement for functions that accept complex configuration objects.

## API Design

The key challenge is creating an API that is both powerful enough to handle complex, nested, and heterogeneous lists, but simple enough that it doesn't require users to write complex wrappers.

The proposed API, which leverages the `specify_*()` function family, distinguishes between checks on the list container and checks on the list elements.

1.  **Container Checks**: Arguments prefixed with a `.` (e.g., `.min_size`, `.max_size`, `.allow_null`) apply to the list object `x` itself.
2.  **Element Checks**: The API uses an explicit set of rules for validating the elements *within* the list `x`:
    * **Required Named Elements**: Named `specify_*` functions passed via `...` define validation rules for list elements that *must* be present by that name (e.g., `title = specify_chr_scalar()`).
    * **Other Named Elements**: The `.named` argument accepts a single `specify_*` function (e.g., `.named = specify_lgl_scalar()`). This function is used to validate all named elements in `x` that are *not* explicitly defined in `...`. If `.named` is `NULL` (the default), any extra named elements will cause an error.
    * **Unnamed Elements**: The `.unnamed` argument accepts a single `specify_*` function (e.g., `.unnamed = specify_int_scalar()`). This function is used to validate *all* unnamed elements in `x`. If `.unnamed` is `NULL` (the default), any unnamed elements will cause an error.

This design allows for precise control over list validation, from strictly defined schemas (the default behavior) to more flexible collections of named or unnamed elements.

## Proposed Signature

```r
stabilize_lst(
  x,
  ..., # Named specify_* functions for required named elements
  .named = NULL, # A single specify_* function for all other named elements
  .unnamed = NULL, # A single specify_* function for all unnamed elements
  .allow_null = TRUE,
  .min_size = NULL,
  .max_size = NULL,
  .x_arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),
  .call = rlang::caller_env(),
  .x_class = object_type(x)
)

# New helper specify_* functions to be created
specify_lst(...)
specify_present(...) (allows any non-NULL)
```

## Concrete Use Case

A function that builds a plot specification can use `stabilize_lst` to validate its main argument. This example uses `specify_lst()` for nested validation.

```r
create_plot_spec <- function(spec) {
  # Define specifications for nested elements first
  spec_aes_coord <- stbl::specify_chr_scalar()
  spec_aesthetics <- stbl::specify_lst(
    x = spec_aes_coord,
    y = spec_aes_coord,
    # .named = NULL (default) errors on other names (e.g., 'color')
    # .unnamed = NULL (default) errors on unnamed elements
    .min_size = 2 # Requires both x and y to be present
  )

  # Define specifications for top-level elements
  spec_title <- stbl::specify_chr_scalar(allow_null = FALSE)
  spec_data <- stbl::specify_present() # Must be present, but can be any non-NULL object

  spec <- stbl::stabilize_lst(
    spec,
    # --- Schema for required list elements ---
    title = spec_title,
    data = spec_data,
    aesthetics = spec_aesthetics,

    # --- Rules for the list container itself ---
    # .named = NULL (default) ensures no extra named elements
    # .unnamed = NULL (default) ensures no unnamed elements
    .min_size = 3 # Ensures all three required elements are present
  )

  # ... function logic now operates on a guaranteed-valid spec object ...
  return(spec)
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- Users can validate that a list contains specific, required named elements and that each element passes its own distinct set of rules.
- Users can validate that all unnamed elements in a list conform to a single, shared set of rules.
- Users can validate that any "extra" named elements (those not explicitly required) all conform to a single, shared set of rules.
- Users can enforce strict list schemas where any elements not explicitly defined (either as required, unnamed, or extra named) will cause an error.
- Users can validate complex, nested list structures (e.g., a list that contains other lists, each with their own validation rules).
- Users can require that a named list element is present and not NULL, without imposing any other type constraints.
- Users can specify the minimum and/or maximum number of elements in a list.
- Users can specify whether a list can be `NULL`.
- Users receive clear, informative error messages that pinpoint which list element failed validation (e.g., "element `title`" or "unnamed element 2") and what rule it violated.
110.comments: Make the main function `stabilize_lst()` (etc), and implement `stabilize_list()` (etc) as aliases, I think. Depending how clean that is, consider writing tickets for more aliases, like `stabilize_character()`.

142.title: Implement `stabilize_df()`

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

142.body:
> As a {stbl} user, in order to validate the structure and content of a data frame, I would like a `stabilize_df()` function.

This function will serve as a wrapper around `stabilize_lst()` (#110) to provide specialized checks for data frames. The function should first verify that the input is a data frame and then use `stabilize_lst()` to apply stabilization rules to each column.

Key features should include:

* **Column stabilization**: Use a named list of stabilization functions to apply to each column.
* **Dimension checks**: Parameters to specify the expected number of rows and columns.
* **Column name checks**: A parameter to ensure that all expected column names are present.

I can imagine an argument to specify what to do with columns that are too short, but I need to conceptualize how that will work and find examples. Check {tidyr}.

178.title: Custom error message helper

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

178.body:
> As a package author, in order to throw more informative error messages, I'd like to customize error messages.

Add a function that wraps `tryCatch()` (or `rlang::try_fetch()`) to catch {stbl} conditions and replace the message (and potentially the class). Make sure it's still able to "remember" where the call is coming from in the error. This will include documentation updates throughout the package to document what different error classes indicate for each function (maybe in `@returns`?).

180.title: Fully implement qcthat
180.body: Once [{qcthat}](https://github.com/Gilead-BioStats/qcthat) hits 1.0.0, set it up completely.

182.title: Implement `to()`

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

182.body:
Add `to()` as a drop-in replacement of `vctrs::vec_cast()`. It's signature should look something like this:

```r
to(
  x,
  .to,
  ...,
  x_arg = caller_arg(x),
  call = caller_env(),
  x_class = object_type(x)
)
```

It dispatches on `.to`, calling the appropriate `to_*()` function. It should be as fast as I can make it. Consider using S7 for the double dispatch, but do a speed comparison to something like `.to_impl(.to, x, ...)` (it's convenient for `x` to be the first argument of `to()`, but we aren't dispatching on it, so reversing them for dispatch might make sense).

One goal for this is to use it in `tibblify()` in place of `rvctrs_vec_cast()` (which is used in `src` to call the `vctrs::vec_cast()` function in R; if we can implement an equivalent of this in C and then dispatch to the appropriate R function, that might be enough for now).

184.title: Profile package and identify C optimization opportunities
184.body:
*Issue created with the assistance of an AI agent.*

> As a maintainer, in order to keep stbl performant as the package grows, I would like to profile the package and identify functions that would benefit from C implementations.

While the current R implementations work well, there may be opportunities to optimize critical functions with C code — both the fast coercion functions (`to_*()`, `is_*_ish()`, `are_*_ish()`) and the comprehensive validation functions (`stabilize_*()`).

**Proposed approach:**

1. **Profile current performance** — Benchmark core functions with representative inputs of varying sizes
2. **Identify bottlenecks** — Determine which functions or operations consume the most time
3. **Prioritize by impact** — Focus on functions where C would provide meaningful speedups
4. **Create specific issues** — File individual issues for each function identified as a good candidate for C optimization

**Success criteria:**
- Documented baseline benchmarks for core functions
- Specific issues created for functions worth optimizing
- Each follow-up issue includes benchmark targets

186.title: feat: to_string()

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

186.body:
## Summary

> As a package developer, in order to make sure an argument contains a non-NULL, non-empty character scalar, I would like a `to_string()` convenience wrapper around `to_chr_scalar()`.

## Proposed signature

```r
to_string(x, x_arg = caller_arg(x), call = caller_env())
```

**Arguments**

- `x` (`any`) — The value to coerce.
- `x_arg` (`character(1)`) — Argument name for error messages. Defaults to the name of `x` in the caller.
- `call` (`environment`) — The call environment for error messages.

**Returns** a `character(1)`: `x` coerced to a non-NULL, non-empty character scalar.

## Details

Implementation:

```r
to_string <- function(x, x_arg = caller_arg(x), call = caller_env()) {
  to_chr_scalar(
    x,
    allow_null = FALSE,
    allow_zero_length = FALSE,
    x_arg = x_arg,
    call = call
  )
}
```

Tests:

```r
test_that("to_string() returns a non-NULL, non-empty character scalar", {
  expect_identical(to_string("hello"), "hello")
  expect_pkg_error_classes(to_string(NULL), "stbl", "bad_null")
})
```

## Behavior

- Delegates entirely to `to_chr_scalar(x, allow_null = FALSE, allow_zero_length = FALSE, x_arg = x_arg, call = call)`.
- Errors with class `bad_null` when `x` is `NULL`.
- Errors with class `bad_empty` when `x` is a zero-length character vector.
- All other coercion rules follow `to_chr_scalar()`.

187.title: feat: to_boolean()

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

187.body:
## Summary

> As a package developer, in order to make sure an argument contains a non-NULL logical scalar that will work in an `if()`, I would like a `to_boolean()` convenience wrapper around `to_lgl_scalar()`.

## Proposed signature

```r
to_boolean(x, x_arg = caller_arg(x), call = caller_env())
```

**Arguments**

- `x` (`any`) — The value to coerce.
- `x_arg` (`character(1)`) — Argument name for error messages. Defaults to the name of `x` in the caller.
- `call` (`environment`) — The call environment for error messages.

**Returns** a `logical(1)`: `x` coerced to a non-NULL logical scalar.

## Details

Implementation:

```r
to_boolean <- function(x, x_arg = caller_arg(x), call = caller_env()) {
  to_lgl_scalar(x, allow_null = FALSE, x_arg = x_arg, call = call)
}
```

Tests:

```r
test_that("to_boolean() returns a non-NULL logical scalar", {
  expect_identical(to_boolean(TRUE), TRUE)
  expect_identical(to_boolean(FALSE), FALSE)
  expect_pkg_error_classes(to_boolean(NULL), "stbl", "bad_null")
})
```

## Behavior

- Delegates entirely to `to_lgl_scalar(x, allow_null = FALSE, x_arg = x_arg, call = call)`.
- Errors with class `bad_null` when `x` is `NULL`.
- All other coercion rules follow `to_lgl_scalar()`.

188.title: feat: expect_pkg_error_snapshot()

type.name: Feature
type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality

188.body:
## Summary

> As a package developer, in order to snapshot-test the full error output of `stbl`-style errors in one step, I would like an `expect_pkg_error_snapshot()` helper that wraps `expect_snapshot()` and `expect_pkg_error_classes()`.

## Proposed signature

```r
expect_pkg_error_snapshot(
  object,
  error_class_component,
  package,
  transform = NULL,
  variant = NULL,
  call = caller_env()
)
```

**Arguments**

- `object` (`expression`) — The expression expected to throw a package error.
- `error_class_component` (`character(1)`) — The error subclass component to check (passed to `expect_pkg_error_classes()`).
- `package` (`character(1)`) — The package name for the error class hierarchy (passed to `expect_pkg_error_classes()`).
- `transform` (`function` or `NULL`) — Optional function to scrub volatile output (e.g. temp paths) before snapshot comparison. Passed through to `expect_snapshot()`.
- `variant` (`character(1)` or `NULL`) — Optional snapshot variant name. Passed through to `expect_snapshot()`.
- `call` (`environment`) — The call environment used as the evaluation environment for `inject()`.

**Returns** the result of `expect_snapshot()`, invisibly.

## Details

Implementation inspired by [api2r/pkgskills](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/api2r/pkgskills/refs/heads/main/tests/testthat/helper-expectations.R):

```r
# `rlang::enexpr()` is used instead of `rlang::enquo()` because `inject()`
# splices a quosure as `^(expr)`, which breaks `expect_snapshot()`'s internal
# `parse(deparse(x))` round-trip. `enexpr()` captures only the bare expression,
# so the snapshot's Code section shows the full inner call transparently.
#
# The `call` parameter (defaulting to `caller_env()`) is forwarded to `inject()`
# as `env =` so that `expect_snapshot()` is evaluated in the test's environment.
# Without this, local variables in the expression (e.g. `tmp`) would be out of
# scope.
#
# The `transform` parameter is forwarded to `expect_snapshot()` to allow callers
# to scrub volatile values (e.g. temp paths) before snapshot comparison.
expect_pkg_error_snapshot <- function(
  object,
  error_class_component,
  package,
  transform = NULL,
  variant = NULL,
  call = caller_env()
) {
  obj_expr <- rlang::enexpr(object)
  transform_expr <- rlang::enexpr(transform)
  rlang::inject(
    expect_snapshot(
      {
        (expect_pkg_error_classes(
          !!obj_expr,
          !!package,
          !!error_class_component
        ))
      },
      transform = !!transform_expr,
      variant = !!variant
    ),
    env = call
  )
}
```

## Behavior

- Wraps `object` in a call to `expect_pkg_error_classes()`, then passes the whole expression to `expect_snapshot()`.
- Uses `rlang::enexpr()` (not `enquo()`) to capture `object` and `transform` as bare expressions, so that `expect_snapshot()`'s internal `parse(deparse(x))` round-trip produces a readable `Code:` section in the snapshot.
- Uses `rlang::inject()` with `env = call` to splice the captured expressions, so local variables in `object` remain in scope.
- `transform` and `variant` are forwarded to `expect_snapshot()`.
- Requires `testthat` (via `rlang::check_installed()`).

189.title: feat: `to_*_scalar()` default arguments
189.body:
## Summary

> As a package developer, in order to make sure an argument contains what I expect, I would like the `to_*_scalar()` functions to default to `allow_null = FALSE` and `allow_zero_length = FALSE`.

## Details

Update the definitions of all `to_*_scalar()` functions to default to  `allow_null = FALSE` and `allow_zero_length = FALSE`. Make sure the tests match this update, and that the shared documentation doesn't specify the default (since these parameters will still default to `TRUE` for the non-scalar versions).

## News

Mention this in @NEWS.md as a breaking change.

190.title: feat: `to_*_scalar()` and `stabilize_*_scalar()` default to `allow_null = FALSE` and `allow_zero_length = FALSE`
190.body:
The ten scalar functions (`to_*_scalar()` and `stabilize_*_scalar()`) previously defaulted to `allow_null = TRUE` and `allow_zero_length = TRUE`, making them permissive by default—opposite to the intent of scalar validation.

## Breaking change

`to_chr_scalar()`, `to_dbl_scalar()`, `to_fct_scalar()`, `to_int_scalar()`, `to_lgl_scalar()`, `stabilize_chr_scalar()`, `stabilize_dbl_scalar()`, `stabilize_fct_scalar()`, `stabilize_int_scalar()`, and `stabilize_lgl_scalar()` (and their synonyms) now reject `NULL` and zero-length inputs by default:

```r
# Previously returned NULL silently; now errors by default
to_chr_scalar(NULL)
#> Error: `x` must not be <NULL>.

stabilize_chr_scalar(NULL)
#> Error: `x` must not be <NULL>.

# Opt back in explicitly
to_chr_scalar(NULL, allow_null = TRUE)
#> NULL

stabilize_chr_scalar(NULL, allow_null = TRUE)
#> NULL
```

## Changes

- **`R/to_*.R`** — All five `to_*_scalar()` signatures updated to `allow_null = FALSE, allow_zero_length = FALSE`.
- **`R/stabilize_*.R`** — All five `stabilize_*_scalar()` signatures updated to `allow_null = FALSE, allow_zero_length = FALSE`.
- **`R/check.R`** — Removed now-redundant explicit `allow_null = FALSE` from internal `to_lgl_scalar()` calls (×3); `to_int_scalar(min_size/max_size, allow_null = TRUE)` retained as these legitimately receive `NULL` when bounds are unspecified.
- **`R/stabilize_dbl.R`** — `to_dbl_scalar(min_value/max_value, allow_null = TRUE)`: same rationale; `NULL` means "no bound".
- **`R/to_dbl.R`, `R/to_int.R`** — Removed redundant `allow_null = FALSE` from `coerce_character` validation calls.
- **`R/cls_unexported.R`** — Removed redundant `allow_null = FALSE` from `coerce_factor` validation call.
- **`R/to_null.R`** — Removed redundant `allow_null = FALSE` from `allow_null` validation call.
- **Tests** — Removed redundant explicit `allow_null = FALSE` / `allow_zero_length = FALSE` from scalar tests (now the default); added `allow_null = TRUE` where `NULL` pass-through is the tested behavior; added `allow_null` and `allow_zero_length` coverage for `to_dbl_scalar()`, `to_int_scalar()`, and all five `stabilize_*_scalar()` functions.
- **Snapshots** — Updated `Code:` sections to reflect new call sites; added new NULL-error snapshots for `stabilize_*_scalar()`.
- **`NEWS.md`** — Documented as a breaking change covering both `to_*_scalar()` and `stabilize_*_scalar()`.
- **`.github/workflows/pr-commands.yaml`** — Added `contents: write` and `pull-requests: write` permissions to the `document` job; removed the `style` job.
